decision.semantic_admission_delta: positive for focused JS/TS Promise.finally settlement fixtures; unchanged on the local crates runtime surface because crates has no JS/TS Promise continuation rows
decision.summary: Open the exact-safe Promise.finally settlement subset through the existing Promise settlement model. A finally continuation can recover only with admitted PromiseLike receiver proof and an absent or zero-argument handler whose result is non-thenable-safe, a fulfilled Promise boundary, or a rejected Promise boundary.
policy: A .finally selector alone is not Promise proof; the call must have admitted Promise.finally LibraryApi evidence and PromiseLike receiver proof.
Safe fulfilled finally handlers preserve the original fulfilled or rejected settlement.
A finally handler returning a rejected Promise boundary overrides the original settlement with that rejected channel.
The recovered value remains behind a Promise boundary and does not converge with synchronous payload code.
Parameterized handlers, possible thenables, selector-only receivers, imported producers without settled-value contracts, constructors, aggregate combinators, and broad async scheduling stay closed.

kernel_capabilities: nose.javascript.builtins.promise now owns a PromiseFinally contract and occurrence row alongside Promise.resolve, Promise.reject, Promise.then, and Promise.catch
Promise continuation recovery consumes PromiseFinally through the same admitted LibraryApi and PromiseLike receiver dependencies as then/catch
the value graph models finally as settlement passthrough for safe fulfilled handler outcomes and as rejection-channel override for rejected handler outcomes
compiled Promise pack summary counts now include reject, catch, and finally contract rows
newly_supported_equivalences: Promise.resolve(1).finally(() => 9).then(x => x + 1) converges with Promise.resolve(1).then(x => x + 1)
Promise.reject(1).finally(() => 9).catch(e => e + 1) converges with Promise.reject(1).catch(e => e + 1)
Promise.resolve(1).finally(() => Promise.reject(2)).catch(e => e + 1) converges with Promise.reject(2).catch(e => e + 1)
still_closed_equivalences: Promise.finally handlers returning possible thenables stay opaque
Promise.finally handlers with parameters stay opaque until undefined-argument semantics are modeled
selector-only or custom finally receivers stay opaque
imported function/member Promise producers still need settled-value contracts
constructors, aggregate combinators, await, and broad scheduling remain closed
reporting_delta: The Promise continuation recall-loss fixture now includes finallyLocal(), a safe local Promise.finally continuation that does not add a runtime-boundary admission rejection.

Raw finallyIt(p, h) still reports promise-finally-settlement-continuation-contract-missing and promise-finally-callback-demand-effect-contract, preserving the existing fail-closed reporting vocabulary.

covered: Promise.finally requires a JS-like surface, arity 1, and ExactPromiseLike receiver contract
Promise.finally admission requires PromiseLike receiver proof
the JavaScript Promise builtin pack summary includes reject, catch, and finally contract rows
literal finally handlers preserve fulfilled values
literal finally handlers preserve rejected values
handler-returned Promise.resolve preserves the original settlement
handler-returned Promise.reject overrides the result channel
unknown handler results and parameterized handlers stay opaque
safe Promise.finally passthrough converges with direct then/catch chains
Promise.finally recovery preserves the Promise boundary
unsafe finally handlers remain distinct from direct Promise continuation forms
safe local finally recovery does not add a runtime-boundary rejection
raw finally continuations still report focused settlement and callback-demand missing evidence

next_work: Design a settled-value contract for imported Promise producers instead of inferring payloads from import target identity.
Price Promise.finally handler shapes in the pinned JS/TS corpus so future recovery can decide whether undefined-argument semantics or additional no-op handler forms are worth modeling.
Keep aggregate combinators and broad async/await scheduling closed until first-settled, all-settled, thenable assimilation, and scheduling obligations are explicit.
